blocks.content: Mara Voss had known Julian Cross for seven years and understood him with the specific expertise of someone who had spent that time translating between him and the rest of the world.

She knew that when he said probably nothing, he meant probably something. She knew that when he didn't sleep, it was because a detail had lodged in him like a splinter — invisible from the outside, insistent from within. She knew that the logbook and the guest patterns and the walk that ended with a face were all the same thing: Julian unable to stop doing what he did even in the absence of a reason to do it.

She also knew, because she was realistic about who she was dealing with, that if something did happen in that house two doors down, there was no version of events in which Julian Cross walked past it.

She lay in her room and looked at the ceiling and thought: maybe nothing will happen. People look at each other strangely at beach bonfires. Context matters. She was projecting Julian's instincts onto a social situation that probably had a completely ordinary explanation.

Six friends at a rental house. Someone had relationship drama, probably. Someone was annoyed about money. Someone was watching an ex's boyfriend. Normal human complicated things that had nothing to do with forensic consultants on forced vacation.
She went to sleep telling herself this.
